author: @Dutchess81
text: My purge tower on the edge of the heat bed kept falling off, which made the actual print stringy, cause the nozzle still had the strings that the purge tower should have had. So thank you for the tip, especially happy that I don't need to buy the more expensive ones. :D

author: @VoidySA
text: A can of cheap hairspray lasts months and you can print a few prints on one squirt. Once cooled the part just pops off the bed, the bed cleans easily with water.

author: @DrSteveMorreale
text: Great tip! Maybe in 2024 I'll get a 3D printer!

author: @3D_Printing
text: Elmer's Purple Glue Stick, is Good, put on cool wait for the Purple colour vanish print about 60 C quite a number of times. Clean off and do again

author: @backyardbasher
text: Glue Stick is a bandage, do yourself a favor and get a PEI sheet, when warm it's thermal properties act like glue keeping tghe print stuck to it. even when cooled down it can be tricky to get the print off. I use a thin paint scraper with a ground edge and place it on the bed at the corner of the print and tap it with something and that lifts the print enough to get the putty knife underneath.

author: @hollylaw8272
text: I use a pei magnetic board and an air shield around my printer. I started out trying glue sticks but they ended up giving me more problems for my board, like residue that was hard to get off and my prints never fully stuck. I also tried the blue tape method... also didn't work well for me. I like my pei board now because it is metal, radiates the heat better to the full board, and it is flexible, so hard sticking projects can be easily gotten off. My projects stick very well without much prep. I love it!

author: @richardglickman7203
text:
I just received my Elegoo Neptune 4 and my first large print curled up on the left and back edges.

The glue would not have helped! The print lifted the magnetic base off the platter!

author: @IfDawidCanYouCan
text:
Brilliant! As I was worrying if I was doing something wrong using glue sticks. Unfortunately, I’m having lot of issues printing on my Ender-3 S1 Pro. I complained to Creality and they sent me another one to replace the faulty one. It started fine, and missing now and then until the Z-axis keep slipping down. So, I checked all the screws and tighten up some obvious loose screws. Now I’m having more drama, levelling the hot bed. Once I manually levelled the bed, and ran the auto levelling the Nozzle keep touching the bed and sticking to a position with lot of noises. While doing that I lowered the bed by tighten up the screws at the four corners to resolve the issue. After getting +/- 0.1 to  +/-0.15 , I started printing but, as soon as the first layer or two, the model keeps touching the nozzle and ruining my print. I’m very new to 3D printing & learning here on this platform from knowledgable YouTubers like yourselves. Now my question to you is, what should I do to resolve this please!
Thank you in advance!!!

Remember,*_Life_* is *_Short_* so *_Live Long_* and *_Prosper_* … 🖖 Cheers!!!

Happy Holidays and a Very Happy New Year 2024…

author: @RickyStenicky2234
text: If you really want to try something different, try sugar water. It works great and is available anywhere. Only works on heated beds.

author: @newolde1
text: For ABS or ASA, you can use a light slurry. Acetone mixed with cuts of your leftover brims and supports. A small drip bottle works nicely to apply. Thin coat with the bed heated to 65c is optimal. Glass or metal bed, not PEI, don't use PEI for ABS, it'll ruin it over time. You can also use the acetone for vapor smoothing and to make glue for parts, which is just a thicker form of the slurry.

author: @AndysArchives68
text: thank you robert another good  tip

author: @chrisbingham3289
text: The same as everyone used it for years, but be warned it's don't always work if I’am doing a  very large print which will take hours to complete I secure the edges with cellotape just in case. Also If I’am not shore how something will fit together or look I will print a small version first . Saved me a fortune in time and filament over the years.

author: @adrianscarlett

author: @geekcorerob
text: Personally, I find that hairspray works well for me.  Aqua Net usually.

author: @VeniceInventors
text:
Interesting how many people use hairspray.
My printer bed doesn't have a heater but it turns out that it's not really necessary. At least on my fiberglass bed, when printing with PLA/PLA+ I stopped having lifted corners and slippery prints by cleaning the plate really well before each print, keeping the room warm, printing at 220C instead of 210 or 200, and only using glue if a given print gave me a hard time before.
My only issue with glue is how difficult it makes it to remove very thin parts, even with warm water, but I haven't tried rubbing alcohol yet.

author: @vintasalo
text:
Get a can of AQUA-NET hair spray, spray the bed, let it heat and dry. Then print! Easier than a glue stick

Other fixes: 
1.Print in an enclosure
2.Print on a raft
3.Print in a climate controlled room

author: @richardclark5112
text: Can the heat from the centre of panel be shared out to the edges by soldering a web of copper wires from heat source to outer edges perhaps?

replies.author: @SianaGearz
replies.text: He is plain mistaken. The heater is the entire area. There's a giant copper serpentine track that covers all the area and gets the current run through it and acts as a heater, measuring about 3-5 Ohm resistance in total. It's connected by two large pads at the corner. The thing in the middle is the temperature probe (TDK glass bead thermistor 100k Beta 3950) and a sticky pad that holds it. I do think the sticky pad is unbalancing the temperature a little, because it prevents the cold air underneath from cooling that exact spot in the middle.

author: @Moogus
text: Personally I had better results with hairspray with my printer.

text: Have you tried placing an alumin(i)um sheet under the glass bed?  I am told that it spreads the heat from the warmer.  Wondering if carbon ink painted on the underside of the bed would also work.

author: @mark_bally
text: The best solution I've found to ensure good adhesion to the build surface is to use green or blue painters tape depending on how much adhesion I need. Generally I go with green painters tape as blue painters tape can be harder to remove print from surface. This can be reprinted on hundreds of times without reapplying if removal of prints is careful and only ripped tape needs replacing.This will provide such good adhesion that the print can fully cool and still remain adhered to the build plate. This gives more consistent results as the better the adhesion to build plate the better your printer will print. For example using tape on build plate, I can print a vase 1 line thick of plastic that can hold water without leaking. If I used glue, I would be luck if after a dozen lines thick the vase could hold water without leaking. Another major improvement in prints came from increasing flow rate for my filament during print, 150% flow rate in my case. But tested using 5% increase per test till I found right flow rate per print as it can range depending on the layout and overhang. The greater the overhang the higher the flow. This will also change thickness of the prints but vastly increase bonding of layers by forcing more pressure from extrudered plastic on previous layer.

replies.author: @SianaGearz

@SianaGearz
replies.text: @CaliMeatWagon  We don't have Aquanet in this region. Not in retail stores, not in retail store web catalogues, not on Amazon, nowhere. Might be a US-local product? There might also be something different between EU hairsprays and US ones regarding commonly used ingredients, all the ones sold are actually of local manufacturing so they might just be basically same 2 products under 20 different brands scents and price points.

author: @JJFX-
text:
For spring steel PEI beds, I'd urge people to only do this as a last resort. With PLA your use case can help but with ABS it can cause more problems than it solves. What you want to do is clean the PEI using a tiny dab of acetone applied to a clean rag. Do NOT squirt it directly on the bed or it could ruin it. If that still doesn't help, lightly buff it with fine steel wool then clean it with the rag again.

This is night and day compared to cleaning with IPA, which can leave a residue. PEI grips very well when prepped properly and the PVA glue can actually give you more trouble. You should definitely use it when printing PETG, especially on glass, to protect it from adhering too well.

author: @gshingles
text:
I started with glue stick and it does work well. As the printer got older I tried tape which also works (blue 3M high temp tape, but that is expensive). So I figured the tape is essentially just paper and glue, so I tried ordinary laser printer paper attached with glue stick. It works really well, sometimes too well. Basically a clean glass bed, cut a square of paper, rub the glue stick on the paper--concentrating on the edges, then attach and smooth over the glass from the center outward. The paper has 'sides', like a rough and a smooth side, but I can't say I've noticed a difference, I try to get the glue on the rough side though. Naturally, you need to re-level the bed and get the right gap to the nozzle.
EDIT: Oh, I also attach the paper when the bed is hot. When the bed cools down it warps, but smooths out again when the bed heats again. I can get quite a few uses out of the paper if the adhesion is just right.

replies.author: @newolde1
replies.text:
Can just use ABS slurry. Mix acetone with cut up scraps of brim and supports in a drip bottle. Apply thin with a bit of heat, 65c. Best with a glass bed, not PEI. But you don't really want to do ABS with PEI anyways it'll ruin it over time. Sticks like no other. You can also use the acetone for smoothing, and make glue, just a thicker form of slurry. 

I've also heard good things about garolite, but haven't tried yet.

author: @peterbrown172
text: I find hairspray works better. It’s water soluble so it also releases quite well.

author: @geepersoilyrag1884
text: I used to use a glue stick, lasts for ages once you have a base layer, just wet it a little and spread it round and its ready to go again for a surprising amount of times. Switched over to blue masking tape though and havent looked back

author: @baldeagle5297
text: I've never had to use glue. My bed has a maze-like copper heating foil that covers the entire bottom surface. It heats evenly. On cold days, I may spritz a little Aqua Net hairspray to help out.

author: @DKFX1
text: I use a special print bed glue spray, which is pricey but works really well. Took me over a year to realize I can reuse each glue coating up to 5-10 times by re-smearing it around the plate with a dab of isopropyl alchohol.

replies.author: @vintasalo
replies.text: Or just use Aqua-net hair spray

author: @perkmeister1
text: 3DLac spray is the stuff you need 😉

author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
text: Been there and done all this even bought enclosures. The issue is not the printer. Its what you are printing, the material you are printing with and some settings that are critical to the type of materials used. Some parts are just going to warp no matter what you do, even inside an enclosure. I started using rafts and now I pretty much use them on all prints.

author: @M4RC0_RD
text: I apply a bit of glue stick then spray some IPA and rub it all over the plate with a microfibre cloth, Neptune 3 pro, works a treat as no matter what I tried I was having bed adhesion issues, I will dive into it further at some point but for now.. it works fantastically

author: @robertmoore8166
text: I use hairspray.

author: @donniewatson9120
text: Thanks for the isopropyl alcohol idea!!!!

author: @chaorrottai
text:
Not needed, print a full bed first layer and adjust the z-offset until you're first layer is correct. 99.99998% of bed adhesion issues are either that the first layer is incorrect or a draft is blowing on your print.

I didn't have consistent success with 3d printing until I stoppped using a glue stick and the bottom surfaces of my prints are better for it.

replies.author: @DKFX1
replies.text: If a little draft can ruin your multi hour print, then it's already a no-brainer to just apply a little glue and not worry about it.

author: @donniewatson9120
text: Glue sticks have been useful to me as well. However, I've found hairspray to be even more effective.

author: @technodrone313
text: my pei coated steel bed has no issues without a silly gluestick. what is this 2015?

author: @SpaceDeviant
text: Started with the glue stick method.  Works, bit of a pain in the ass to clean up, wasn't 100%.  Ended up switching to a PEI, as long as I didn't mess up the level, never had a fail.  If anything I have had to heat it up and cool it to remove some prints.

author: @ianharvey3696
text: 3DLac Adhesive Spray. On glass surface, cleaned with alcohol and a glass cleaning cloth which has been allowed to dry, no dust...

author: @cwebs1000
text: I have been using glue sticks for three years and found that you don;t need much. I rub some on in the middle and take a damp paper towel and wipe it all around until I have only a haze over the table. That is all that is needed and works great. Sometimes it is a little hard to get the print off the table. FWIW Carl

replies.author: @1234fishnet
replies.text:
I don't wash the beef it after the print. I just add some drops of destilled water and use (always the same) sponge to redistribute the glue.
Works very well on glass. Haven't needed it on a textured/smooth PEI Steel plate yet

author: @Ucceah
text:
over the years i've seen and tried so many variations, most of which worked! mostly still going old school on glass, to this day. 
-plain glass, cleaned with alhohol works decently already, but for extra adhesion rub the bed down with vinegar or spit. 
-glue stick (classic!)
-hair spray
-dliluted PVA wood glue
-blue 3M masking tape. for tricky materials like some TPUs, you can take of the waxy coating with alcohol. 
-for stuff that wont even stick to blue tape, mostly TPU, TPE and PE, you can print straight onto double sided sticky tape.

PS: what also works wonders for big PLA and PETG parts, is printing a thin first layer and including a wide flange of 10-15mm. and isolating the bottom of the plate with depron or corrugated cardboard. saves energy, and it heats up faster and more evenly.

author: @MrFujack103
text: I use pva glue watered down in a spray bottle or rub it over with a sponge to create a haze. This works great and leaves you with a very shiny flat Finish on the bottom of your print. Very economical as you only need a few drops in about 250 mils of water. You should last for ages.

author: @lagunafishing
text: You can also build an enclosure for your machines to keep the draft out and help maintain the ambient temperature.

author: @Andrew-ci1dg
text: Best YouTuber.

author: @ColinWatters
text: I've used glue stick and can confirm it worked well for me, but it's a bit messy to get a nice good smooth layer. I eventually switched to 3DLac which I found easier to apply and dries faster.

author: @earloosthuizenfocus2framep836
text: While the glue sticks do work, I have switched to hairspray. I find it works better for my large printer (Creality CR10 MAX) The glue stick tends to dry out on prolonged prints, but the hairspray becomes more tacky. To remove your print, wait for the bed to cool down, and it comes off easily.

author: @Killianwsh

author: @curtraymond6717
text: I use cheap pancake syrup. It stays tacky and can be spread in a thin, even layer.

author: @silverpc4611
text: Part of the reason a print may lift is the outside air is much cooler. One solution would be to grab some cardboard and duck tape and construct a box around the printer to retain the heat inside. I did this and have no need for a glue stick.

author: @edwardhughes352

author: @nickcalthrop4779
text: I tried a lot of glue sticks whilst printing with ASA over the summer. Pritstick was my least favourite, as I could not lay it down evenly on the bed. Tesco's home brand glue stick worked the best for me.

replies.author: @newolde1
replies.text: For ABS and ASA you can just use a slurry of acetone mixed with your scrap brims and support cut into small pieces. It will dissolve so you can drip and spread onto your bed, best at around 70c. Works better than glue stick. Do not do this with PEI though. Glass bed.

author: @richbuilds_com
text:
Big prints need an enclosure. Just a cardboard box around them is usually enough to keep the heat in. Obviously, the better the insulation, um, the better. 

The *big* printers have extra heaters to keep the build *area* hot, not just the plate.

author: @robertsmith4681
text: Very similar to the way I "learned" to use mine. It took about 5kg of material before I had done enough random "test prints" to get what I consider a "good enough" understanding of the limitations of my unit and then I was off to the races. It is, however a never ending learning experience as each print comes with it's own unique challenges and each roll of filament ends up slightly different than the one before, a simple change in color can have major effects on the results and requires a tweak of settings..

replies.text: There are a lot of good videos explaining the settings, what they mean, and how to adjust them. A good place to start is tutorials on printing gaming miniatures. These guys take their settings seriously.
